## Series of courses

<br />

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=5%}
:::

::: {.column width=95%}

{{< fa 1 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Introduction to Quarto

{{< fa 2 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Advanced Quarto (callout blocks, tabsets, paramaters, citations, cross-references, extensions)

{{< fa 3 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Websites

{{< fa 4 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Blogs

{{< fa 5 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Slides

{{< fa 6 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Publish w/ GitHub Pages

:::
::::


## Series of courses

<br />

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=5%}
:::

::: {.column width=95%}

{{< fa 1 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
**Introduction to Quarto**

{{< fa 2 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Advanced Quarto (callout blocks, tabsets, paramaters, citations, cross-references, extensions)

{{< fa 3 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Websites

{{< fa 4 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Blogs

{{< fa 5 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Slides

{{< fa 6 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Publish w/ GitHub Pages

:::
::::

# Motivations

## A common problem

In data science, we often separate:

- code
- results
- explanatory text

<br/>

. . .

This creates several issues:

- lack of reproducibility
- hard maintenance
- results becoming disconnected from the code


## A common problem

::: {.center}

[{{< video https://www.youtube.com/embed/s3JldKoA0zw width="800" height="450" >}}]{.center}

[Credits: [Ignasi Bartomeus](https://bartomeuslab.com/)]{.small}
:::


## Literate programming

Paradigm introduced in 1984 by [Donald Knuth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Knuth)[^tex]

<br />


> Literate programming is an approach in which code,  results and narrative 
> are written together in the same document, so that programs can be understood 
> by humans as well as executed by computers.

[^tex]: He created the typesetting program [TeX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TeX)

. . .

<br />

::: {.boxed .center}
**Reproducibility**
:::

. . .

<br /><br />

{{< fa circle-arrow-right >}}&nbsp;
[**Quarto**](https://quarto.org/) and [**R Markdown**](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/) are modern implementations of this paradigm



# Quarto

## What is Quarto?

- Quarto is an open-source scientific and technical **publishing system** designed to create 
reproducible documents, websites, presentations, books, and dashboards

- Quarto is a **CLI software** supporting different languages (R, Python, Julia & Observable JS)

- Quarto is a powerful, flexible and multi-language **successor of R Markdown**

- Quarto supports dozens of output formats: HTML, PDF, Word, Slide, eBook, Website, etc.

<br />

![Credits: [Allison Horst](https://allisonhorst.com/)](images/quarto-outputs.png){fig-align='center' width=60%}

## How it works?

1. You write a Quarto document (`.qmd` file) with narrative, metadata and code chunks
1. Convert this document in a Markdown (`.md`) file using `knitr`[^knitr]
1. Convert this `.md` file in the final output using `Pandoc`[^pandoc]

<br />

![Credits: [Allison Horst](https://allisonhorst.com/)](images/quarto-machinery.png){fig-align='center' width=60%}

[^knitr]: The R package `knitr` processes R code chunks, executes them, and inserts the results into the Markdown document.
[^pandoc]: Quarto comes with a built-in and modified version of `Pandoc`


## Quarto vs R Markdown


| Feature / Aspect              | R Markdown                             | Quarto                                                     |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| **File extension**            | `.Rmd`                                 | `.qmd`                                                     |
| **Primary language support**  | R                                      | R, Python, Julia, Observable JS                            |
| **Execution engine for R**    | `knitr`                                | `knitr` (for R)                                            |
| **Other languages**           | Limited (Python via `reticulate`)      | Jupyter kernels (Python, Julia, etc.)                      |
| **Output formats**^1^         | HTML, PDF, Word                        | HTML, PDF, Word, Reveal.js, Beamer, PowerPoint, dashboards |
| **Project types**^1^          | Individual documents                   | Documents, websites, books, dashboards                     |
| **Cross-references**^1^       | Limited, requires `bookdown` or manual | Built-in support for figures, tables, sections             |
| **Citations / bibliography**  | `bibliography` in YAML, Pandoc syntax  | Pandoc-style, built-in support                             |
| **Themes & styling**^1^       | Limited                                | Multiple themes, CSS/HTML customization                    |
| **Interactive features**      | Shiny (R only)                         | Shiny (R), Observable JS, Python widgets                   |
| **Command-line rendering**    | Limited                                | Full support via `quarto render`                           |

: **Table:** Quick comparison of R Markdown & Quarto

<br /><br />

^**1**^ Capabilities of R Markdown can be extended with additional R packages (`distill`, `bookdown`, `blogdown`, `xaringan`, etc.)
but each plugin comes with its own specificity.

<br />

{{< fa circle-arrow-right >}}&nbsp;
Quarto is an unified system


## Installation

Quarto is available for different IDE:

![<https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/>](images/quarto-ide.png){fig-align='center' width=40%}

<br />

- If you use RStudio or Positron IDE, Quarto is already installed
- You also need to install both `knitr` and `rmarkdown` R packages


# The `.qmd` file

## Anatomy of a `.qmd` file

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}


````markdown
---
title: "Hello Quarto"
author: "Jane DOE"
date: "2026-05-13"
format: html
---

# Introduction

Quarto is the next-generation replacement for `R Markdown`, and it
can run **R**, **Python**, **Julia**, and **JavaScript** code.


## R Code Example

Here's a simple R code chunk:


```{{r}}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

summary(cars)
```

End of the demo.
````
:::

::: {.column width=5O%}
:::

::::



## Anatomy of a `.qmd` file

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}


````{.markdown code-line-numbers="1-6"}
---
title: "Hello Quarto"
author: "Jane DOE"
date: "2026-05-13"
format: html
---

# Introduction

Quarto is the next-generation replacement for `R Markdown`, and it
can run **R**, **Python**, **Julia**, and **JavaScript** code.


## R Code Example

Here's a simple R code chunk:


```{{r}}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

summary(cars)
```

End of the demo.
````
:::

::: {.column width=5%}
:::

::: {.column width=45%}
**YAML header**

- Surrounded by `---`
- Describes document metadata
- Controls the output format
- Syntax: `key: value`
:::

::::


## Anatomy of a `.qmd` file

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}


````{.markdown code-line-numbers="8-16,26"}
---
title: "Hello Quarto"
author: "Jane DOE"
date: "2026-05-13"
format: html
---

# Introduction

Quarto is the next-generation replacement for `R Markdown`, and it
can run **R**, **Python**, **Julia**, and **JavaScript** code.


## R Code Example

Here's a simple R code chunk:


```{{r}}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

summary(cars)
```

End of the demo.
````
:::

::: {.column width=5%}
:::

::: {.column width=45%}
**Formatted text**

- The narrative of the document
- Use the markdown syntax
  - Headers w/ `#`, `##`, `###`, etc.
  - Format w/ `**bold**`, `_italic_`, `` `verbatim` ``
  - Hyperlink w/ `[](url)`
  - Image insertion w/ `![caption](image_path)`

<br/>
{{< fa circle-arrow-right >}}&nbsp;
Learn more about the Markdown syntax [here](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html)
:::

::::


## Anatomy of a `.qmd` file

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}


````{.markdown code-line-numbers="19-24"}
---
title: "Hello Quarto"
author: "Jane DOE"
date: "2026-05-13"
format: html
---

# Introduction

Quarto is the next-generation replacement for `R Markdown`, and it
can run **R**, **Python**, **Julia**, and **JavaScript** code.


## R Code Example

Here's a simple R code chunk:


```{{r}}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

summary(cars)
```

End of the demo.
````
:::

::: {.column width=5%}
:::

::: {.column width=45%}
**Code chunks**

- Surrounded by three backticks ` ``` `
- Specify the language w/ ` ```r `
- Specify the language & execute the chunk w/ ` ```{r} `
- Define chunk options w/ `#| key: value`
  - `#| echo: true`
  - `#| eval: false`


<br/>
{{< fa circle-arrow-right >}}&nbsp;
Learn more about chunk options [here](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html)

:::

::::


## Render the output

- Click on the **Render** button of RStudio
- On a terminal, run: `quarto render filename.qmd`
- Use the shortcut `Ctrl + Shift + K`
- With the R package [quarto](https://quarto-dev.github.io/quarto-r/), run `quarto::render("filename.qmd")`

![Credits: [Allison Horst](https://allisonhorst.com/)](images/quarto-workflow.png){fig-align='center' width=60%}


## Render the output

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}


````{.markdown}
---
title: "Hello Quarto"
author: "Jane DOE"
date: "2026-05-13"
format: html
---

# Introduction

Quarto is the next-generation replacement for `R Markdown`, and it
can run **R**, **Python**, **Julia**, and **JavaScript** code.


## R Code Example

Here's a simple R code chunk:


```{{r}}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

summary(cars)
```

End of the demo.
````
:::

::: {.column width=5%}
:::

::: {.column width=45%}
![](images/quarto-render.png){wdith=100% fig-align="center"}
:::

::::



# Figures


## From an external file

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=50%}
In **Pandoc's Markdown**, the basic syntax to insert a figure is:

```markdown
![Caption of the figure](relative_path_to_the_file.png)
```
:::
::::
  

<br />

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=50%}
**Quarto** provides some options to quickly customize figures

```markdown
![This is not a pipe](pipe.png){width=50% fig-align="center"}
```
:::
::::

<br />

. . .

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width=50%}
We can cross-reference figures by adding an ID with the `#fig-*` prefix 

```markdown
![This is not a pipe](pipe.png){#fig-pipe}
```
Then, we can refer to this figure by using `@fig-*`

```markdown
This statement is illustrated well by @fig-pipe.
```
:::
::::


## Advanced layouts


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}
**Figure panel**

```markdown
::: {layout-ncol=2}

![Surus](surus.png)

![Hanno](hanno.png)


:::
```

<br/>

![](images/figure-panel.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
:::

::::


## Advanced layouts


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}
**Figure panel**

```markdown
::: {layout-ncol=2}

![Surus](surus.png)

![Hanno](hanno.png)


:::
```

<br/>

![](images/figure-panel.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
**Subfigures**

```markdown
::: {#fig-elephants layout-ncol=2}

![Surus](surus.png){#fig-surus}

![Hanno](hanno.png){#fig-hanno}

Famous Elephants
:::
```

<br/>

![](images/subfigures.png){fig-align="center"}

:::

::::


## Advanced layouts


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}
**Multiple Rows**

```markdown
::: {layout-nrow=2}
![Surus](surus.png)

![Hanno](hanno.png)

![Abdul Abbas](abdul-abbas.png)

![Lin Wang](lin-wang.png)
:::
```

![](images/multirow-figures.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width=10%}
:::

::: {.column width=40%}
<br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br />

{{< fa circle-arrow-right >}}&nbsp;
Learn more about figures [here](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/figures.html)
:::

::::


## Figures produced by code chunks

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: fig-airquality
#| fig-cap: "Temperature and ozone level"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-align: center

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(airquality, aes(Temp, Ozone)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "loess")
```

````
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
:::

::::


# Tables

## Markdown Tables


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}

````markdown
| Default | Left | Right | Center |
|---------|:-----|------:|:------:|
| 12      | 12   |    12 |   12   |
| 123     | 123  |   123 |  123   |
| 1       | 1    |     1 |   1    |

: This is Markdown table {#tbl-mdtable}
````
:::

::: {.column width=50%}

:::

::::


## Markdown Tables


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}

````markdown
| Default | Left | Right | Center |
|---------|:-----|------:|:------:|
| 12      | 12   |    12 |   12   |
| 123     | 123  |   123 |  123   |
| 1       | 1    |     1 |   1    |

: This is Markdown table {#tbl-mdtable}
````
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
| Default | Left | Right | Center |
|---------|:-----|------:|:------:|
| 12      | 12   |    12 |   12   |
| 123     | 123  |   123 |  123   |
| 1       | 1    |     1 |   1    |

: This is Markdown table {#tbl-mdtable}
:::

::::


<br />

{{< fa circle-arrow-right >}}&nbsp;
Learn more about tables [here](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/figures.html)


## Tables produced by code chunks


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: tbl-cars
#| tbl-cap: "Overview of the cars data.frame"

cars |> 
  head() |> 
  knitr::kable()
```

````
:::

::: {.column width=50%}
:::

::::


# Resources

## Resources

- [Quarto website](https://quarto.org)
- [Quarto guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/)
- [Markdown basics](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html)
- [Code chunk options (1)](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html)
- [Code chunk options (2)](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/execution-options.html)
- [Figures w/ Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/figures.html)
- [Tables w/ Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/tables.html)
- [Quarto for data science](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/quarto.html)

# Next time

## Series of courses

<br />

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=5%}
:::

::: {.column width=95%}

{{< fa 1 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Introduction to Quarto

{{< fa 2 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
**Advanced Quarto (callout blocks, tabsets, paramaters, citations, cross-references, extensions)**

{{< fa 3 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Websites

{{< fa 4 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Blogs

{{< fa 5 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Quarto projects: Slides

{{< fa 6 >}}&nbsp;&nbsp;
Publish w/ GitHub Pages

:::
::::
